verses.text: Ahasuerus, ruling from India to Ethiopia, holds a half-year display of his wealth and majesty before his nobles. Earthly power loves to parade its glory. Yet all this splendour is the stage on which God, unnamed in this book, will quietly work to save His people.

A seven-day feast follows in the palace garden, with lavish hangings and wine served without limit. The king spares no cost in pleasure. Worldly greatness often spends itself on show, little dreaming how its excess may serve a higher purpose.

Flushed with wine, the king commands Queen Vashti to parade her beauty before the drunken lords; she refuses, and he burns with anger. A proud demand meets a firm refusal. God uses even a royal quarrel, sprung from wine and vanity, to begin His hidden plan.

The king turns to his wise men, learned in law and custom, to decide what should be done with the defiant queen. A trivial slight is treated as grave statecraft. Pride magnifies every offence and drags counsellors into its wounded vanity.

The king turns to his wise men, learned in law and custom, to decide what should be done with the defiant queen. A trivial slight is treated as grave statecraft. Pride magnifies every offence and drags counsellors into its wounded vanity.

Memucan warns that Vashti's act will embolden every wife in the realm, and urges a decree to depose her and give her place to another. A small matter is blown into an empire's crisis. Men's overblown fears become, in God's hand, the very opening for His purpose.

The decree goes out that every man should rule his own house. The king vents his anger in law. Little does he know that in emptying Vashti's throne he is making room for the queen through whom God will save the Jews.
